summary: I first felt part of a larger movement when the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, representatives came into Albany, Georgia. They really infiltrated our community, connecting with social, civic, and religious groups, which was unusual for our small town. Their presence made us realize we were part of a nationwide struggle for civil rights. SNCC brought energy and a new sense of purpose to Albany during the early 1960s.

Albany was a semi-rural town in Georgia with about 50,000 people back then. It was totally segregated—blacks had no political office, no jobs downtown as clerks or salespersons, and no police officers. Most black professionals were teachers, ministers, or a few doctors, and we had one black lawyer, which was rare. Life was segregated by race, and many people had just accepted the status quo, though the community was close-knit and dependent on farming.

Charles Sherrod was one of the SNCC outsiders who came into Albany. He looked like a typical college kid, full of excitement and dedication. He was dynamic, well-spoken, and motivated to make a name for himself and SNCC by working in Albany. The community received him well, sensing he was destined to make a significant impact not just locally but nationally.

The Albany Movement formed spontaneously after SNCC students arrived and stirred things up in the city. Local civic and social leaders, realizing the people were ready for change but they hadn't led yet, decided to come together and guide the movement. We met at Dr. Ed Hamilton's home and agreed to unite under the name 'Albany Movement' to push for desegregation. It was a pivotal moment in Albany's civil rights struggle in the early 1960s.

Dr. Ed Hamilton was a dentist in Albany who played a key role in the civil rights movement. It was at his home that the first meeting of local leaders took place to form the Albany Movement. He was one of the respected community figures who helped bring together various organizations to push for desegregation. His involvement symbolized the local leadership stepping up to guide the movement alongside the outsiders.

Laurie Pritchett was the police chief in Albany during the movement. He once claimed Albany wasn’t that segregated because they had a Catholic mayor and a Jewish judge. But when I spoke of segregation, I meant the racial segregation between blacks and whites, which was total. Pritchett’s perspective missed the point that religious or ethnic minorities like Jews could assimilate more easily than blacks could in Albany at that time.

Asa Kelley was the mayor of Albany and chaired the City Council when we presented our petition for desegregation. At the council meeting, he announced they had no interest in discussing our petition and abruptly adjourned. His response showed the city’s unwillingness to engage with the demands for change. That rejection was one of the triggers that led to mass demonstrations and arrests in Albany.

The Albany Herald was the local newspaper, edited by Mr. Gray, who was also the State Democratic Chairman at the time. After I attended the City Council meeting and asked about our petition, the Herald ran a front-page article portraying me as storming out and demanding total desegregation. They even published my full name and phone number, which exposed me to potential danger. The paper’s coverage escalated tensions just as the Freedom Riders arrived in Albany.

The Freedom Riders came into Albany on a Sunday and were immediately arrested as they got off the train. Their arrival coincided with the Albany Movement’s efforts to desegregate the city. We decided not to let them stay in jail alone and planned mass demonstrations to fill the jails. This was a turning point that led to hundreds being arrested and increased national attention on Albany.

We called in Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. when the Albany Movement realized we were in over our heads with mass arrests and no experience in organizing large-scale protests. I knew Dr. King personally through my wife, who had been his high school classmate, and his close friendship with her brother. After unanimous agreement, I contacted him and he agreed to come help us. His involvement brought much-needed expertise to our struggle in Albany.

David T. Howard High School in Atlanta was where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and my wife’s brother were classmates and close friends. My wife herself had been a high schoolmate of Dr. King there. This school connected us personally to Dr. King and helped facilitate his involvement in the Albany Movement. It was a meaningful link between our local struggle and the broader civil rights leadership.

The Congress of Racial Equality, or CORE, was active in Albany around the same time as SNCC and the Freedom Riders. CORE’s involvement helped bring national attention to the civil rights issues in Albany. Their presence alongside SNCC and the Albany Movement created a complex dynamic, as each organization wanted to maintain its own identity and integrity. Together, they contributed to the momentum for desegregation.

While Ralph Abernathy was a prominent civil rights leader during this era, he was not directly mentioned in my experience with the Albany Movement. However, as a close associate of Dr. King and a key figure nationally, his work paralleled the efforts we were making locally. His leadership helped shape the broader movement that influenced Albany’s struggle for desegregation.

Bernard Lafayette was one of the SNCC activists involved in the civil rights movement at the time, though he was not specifically mentioned in my direct experience. SNCC members like him helped energize communities such as Albany and played important roles in organizing protests and voter registration. Their efforts were part of the larger wave of activism that swept through the South in the early 1960s.

Andrew Young was a significant figure in the civil rights movement and a close ally of Dr. King, though he was not directly referenced in my account of the Albany Movement. His work in organizing and leadership helped support efforts like ours in Albany. Young’s contributions were part of the broader network of activists striving for racial equality during that period.
